data.text:
INT: --eight with Mark Verstandig. 

MV: Yeah. Well, I'll tell you why it was. It must have been the end of '24. The reason is that one and a half years later, there was a bloodless putsch, that was this year. He went with his soldiers and officers of the parliament and sent the Endeks packing. 

And they took over. And the Prime Minister, at that time, by him established, was Mr. Bartel, Kazimierz Bartel. He promised a lot. But this Grabski business died with, with this putsch, anyhow. 

But, but what I am driving at to say is that antisemitism in Poland was instigated by the government, by leaders of the antisemitic parties, who manipulated the Polish population. And that has always been the case. 

INT: And was there any-- in your view, was there any role played by the Church in this? 

MV: The Church was always anti-Jewish. The reason is, first of all, that the Church, that the dominated elements in the Church were the village priests, which were not educated. They were really ignorant people, ignorant people. 

And they, they believed literally in what was written in the-- in the Bible. And they kept the Jews responsible for the crucifixion of Christ. They did whatever they could-- they did whatever they could to incite the Poles against, against the Jews. 

For instance, Good Friday, [NON-ENGLISH]-- in Poland, [NON-ENGLISH] was not a great holiday. It was a fast. But it was not the same as, as Good Friday in the, in the Protestant countries. 

But Jews were afraid to walk the streets of the shtetl or even-- or even of the-- especially of the shtetl. Because, at that time, the priests incited them that the Jews, up till today, until the end of the world, the Jews took upon themselves, according to Matthew, the Jews took upon themselves the responsibility for the blood spilled, for the Christ blood spilled by them, and so on and so on. 

And there was-- there was tension, tension. And it was not a pleasant atmosphere. But again, again, I would say the population, as such, was not very much moved. 

But there was-- there was this-- there was tension. The trouble is that it was Hitler who brutalized, to a very great extent, the whole nation. But up till the war, up till the war, I couldn't see. The only thing there were-- surely, there were pogroms. In Przemysl, there was the pogrom. 

There were in Radom and in many other places. But they were always instigated by people who wanted to have a pogrom. And the people were-- the people were very-- very easily manipulated when it was a question of looting. 

And that is-- that is what happened in Przytyk in 1937. The Poles, the, the farmers came along, the peasants came along, instigated by, by people who, who told them, they say, we'll have an easier life. They-- they will have an easy jobs, to make a pogrom and the-- the-- to, to, and to loot the merchandise in the, in the marketplace. 

The Jews used to have stalls in the marketplace with merchandise. So they said to them that they will-- they will have-- they will make a commotion, and they will grab whatever they could. 

But they never realized that the stall owners, young people, will turn around and will organize themselves, immediately, for a self-defense in Przytyk From the stalls, they grabbed out whatever they could-- poles and so on. And they started to defend themselves. 

And it was that moment, the people ran away. But being on the outskirts of Przytyk they were turned back by these inciters, by, by these manipulators, including Polish police. Go back. 

And this is how the pogrom started. They came back, right like that, and they started to kill the Jews. And when, in the end-- in, in the end, when they were hitting-- they were hitting the doors, they were trying to break the doors of one house, there was, upstairs, one Jew. He has-- he had a rifle. And he shoot and killed a Pole. 

And with that moment, everybody ran away. There was no more pogrom. There were-- there were quite a number of Jews killed-- a number of Jews killed. 

And they prepared a case for the court. The court was in Radom. A famous Jewish journalist and sociologist, Jakob Jasinszki, a great scholar, was, on behalf of the American press, he was recording this trial. 

I knew him very well, by the way. And so what happened was they did then-- the Polish prosecution said that it was the Jews who made the pogrom against the Poles, who came to town in order to feed the Jews. Because they came here to buy the goods and to-- to make possible that the Jews could make a living. 

And instead, they were hit by the Jews. And the end, there were a few people, one family-- one who, who, who killed the-- who killed the Jew and his wife and the children, I think. I don't remember exactly. He was sentenced to a few months, some months of imprisonment. 

There was quite a number of followers, a number of followers-- small number of followers, who were sentenced to, somehow, to some months of imprisonment. But the Jews who defended themselves were sentenced to years of prison, many years of prison. That was, that, that was true. 

But, but that's not-- that was in 1937. The main trouble came-- of course, as I mentioned to you, there were instigators. There were Falangists. There were Endeks. There were the Polish had a Chrzescijanska Demokracja, Christian Democrats. 

A Christian Democrat meant that Christian democracy, that democracy in Poland is only for Christians. That is what they said of-- what they said. I mean, we want democracy, but only for Christians. 

And not only Christians, only for Catholics. They don't want for the Ukrainians and so on, only, only for Polish Christians. 

But the main problem came in 1934. Up till 1934, there were-- it was antisemitism here and there. The Poles still behaved decently, with an exception of the, of the instigators. 

But in 1934, Pilsudski made a pact with Hitler. And too, it said that Hitler's influence on Polish politics, on Polish internal politics got hold. Then now after, the Falangists, they all started openly to be anti-Jew, to, to pay anti-Jewish excesses. 

And what's more, the government did not say no. Then came this new party. Pilsudski died a year later. New party, OZN, Obóz Zjednoczenia Narodowego, something like that. 

And this was already a fascist party. The government party became a fascist party. And after Przytyk, it was General Slawoj Skladkowski, who officially said, I will not allow any pogroms. But economic boycott is pleasure. 

[NON-ENGLISH] yeah, in Poland. Yeah, but how do you say in Polish? [NON-ENGLISH]. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. They used the excuse of [NON-ENGLISH], economic boycott [NON-ENGLISH]. It means it's, it's accepted. 

And it was that time they tried to make, they decide to really implement it. How could they implement it? How could they have implemented it? 

The only way was to bring Polish merchants from Pomerania, from, from Poznan, and this western part of Poland, where there always were antisemites, although this was the place where no-- where the Jews didn't live. That's very important. 

The antisemites, the real antisemites, Poles, came from the western part of Poland-- Pomerania. And Jews didn't live there. So they organized in a-- in my-- a migration of Polish merchants, from Western Poland, from Pomerania, to Galicia and to all other parts of Poland, to take over the Jewish trades, Jewish, Jewish, Jewish shops. 

Now, they did it with large credits. And I still remember, in my, in my, in my town, there were two cafes. They opened two cafes. This was people they, they bought from Pomerania, from Posen, from Poznan. 

They opened two cafes. And that was the first time a cafe put up a sign-- Jews are not permitted to come in. And I remember, Jews were just bewildered, going over to see this sign. 

INT: What year was that? 

MV: Huh? 

INT: What year was that? What year was that? 

MV: That was in 1937, 193-- end of 1936, '37. 1937. Either, there were-- this, this cafe, this restaurant, cafe did not do too good. There was another cafe, by a man from the same Pomerania, who did well and invited Jews. 

I used to go there. He was my client. He was my client. He was-- we, we-- he-- we did all, all the legal work for them. 

INT: Can we just go back, quickly, to the end of your university years. What year did you finish university? 

MV: Beginning of 19-- 1936, middle 1936. 

INT: And you mentioned earlier that one of the student parties was from the far right. Another one represented Pilsudski's. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: What were-- were there any other major-- 

MV: Sanacja. 

INT: Were there any other major student parties? 

MV: No. There were-- yes. There were [NON-ENGLISH], the peasant party. But they were quite-- they were not a large-- they were not a large, large amount of them. 

Mostly, they were either Sanacja or they were Endeks, or they were Endeks. As a matter of fact, in the Sanacja students, put it frankly, they were not people-- to put it that way-- in-- who would be friends of Jews. They were mostly opportunists. 

They didn't believe that the Endeks will take over the government. And they studied at the university, thinking that being Sanacja, being the organization of students representing, represented by the government, they will get good jobs. 

So they were mostly opportunists. So it was-- this is the, the party which they created later, the [NON-ENGLISH]. That means the, the non-party bloc supporting the government. 

They were all opportunists, because they thought that being with the party, being with the party of the government, they will have better jobs. They will be better. They will be able to get a better loan from a government bank. 

INT: Were the Jewish students organized? 

MV: The Jewish students were organized. The Jewish students, we organized. And then we organized most-- first of all, there was the Jewish Student Union, [NON-ENGLISH], officially. 

For instance, m this beautiful house-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- which you would say, it was the student house. It was partly, quite in a large extent, funded by the university. The university pays a lot of money for helping, see? They're helping to establish it. They couldn't take-- according to the Constitution, they couldn't have done otherwise. Because they were-- there were similar houses for Catholics, and there were similar houses for Protestants, even, for-- 'cause, 'cause-- because they were German students. 

They couldn't say that they will not give some money for the Jewish students. So that meant that house was partly funded by the university. It was, just was a student union in Cracow called [NON-ENGLISH], 'wreath of fire.' 

[NON-ENGLISH], you would say, well, you would say, it is a fire. 

INT: Were you involved in the Jewish-- 

MV: Oh yes, oh yes. I was involved, but not in the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was involved in the political Zionist parties. 

INT: What was that name? 

MV: The Zionist-- first of all, after the second year, I was invited to take over the academic-- the university students' organization, called [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the revisionist student Jabotinsky's group. 

INT: And what was its philosophy? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: What was its philosophy? 

MV: Philosophy was, it, it was Zionists for a, for, for a Jewish state, for a Jewish state in Palestine, for a large Jewish state in Palestine, against the, the immigration, the immigration-- against the English prohibition of immigration. 

It was, it was representing Jewish Jabotinsky's philosophy at the campus. I would have to start with Jabotinsky's philosophy altogether. 

It was, he, he-- Jabotinsky was the man who, at that time, traveled along Poland, from shtetl to shtetl, warning Jews that the catastrophe comes, that the Jews will not survive the next war, that the Hitler rise, the Jewish will not survive that. 

And he, he, he went. He made-- he went at-- he made him an agreement with the Polish government, because England did not allow to-- so they did not-- England did not allow the Jews to migrate to, to Palestine. There were very strict quotas. 

Quota was the, the so-called certificates or visas were issued through a Jewish agency, but there were a very small number. And then, you could go on as a capitalist, with 5,000 pounds or $5,000-- I don't remember the amount, the amount of money. 

And besides this, they didn't allow anybody to go in, to go in. Now, he makes an agreement with the Polish government. And the Polish government was only willing to help Jews go to Palestine. And so they smuggled him, at that time, through Romania into Palestine. 

Because in Palestine, on the other hand-- when they, when he arrived in Palestine, the, the illegal immigrant when he arrives there, he didn't need any papers. It's like here and England, you don't need-- you don't need any papers. 

They came there, and they disappeared in the kibbutzim. They disappeared amongst the Jewish-- amongst the Jewish population. Anyhow, a lot of people were sent illegally, illegally to, to Palestine through the Jabotinsky movement. 

And they were for a Jewish state at the time, when the Jewish agency, or the Zionist-- the main Zionist organization, they said that they would be willing for to settle for a Jewish entity within Palestine. 

Well, we said, no. We are not going to kill-- not going to kill anybody. We are going. We are going there to have a state of ours. And now, this was more or less there. And Jabotinsky traveled from town to town, calling on Jews. 

INT: What year was that? 

MV: 1936, '37, '38. 

INT: Did you agree with that prognosis? 

MV: If I agreed? Of what? 

INT: Did you think the same way, did you think that the disaster might come? 

MV: The same way. I thought the same way, but later, I changed my mind. I, at first, I thought that we-- we are going to stay here, and we are going to fight for our, for our rights, here. 

But this was when I was in that university. At the university, we are, we were then, at that time, fighting for a Jewish-- for a Jewish state against the English restrictions to go to, to go to Palestine. 

Now, I became the president of this organization in Cracow. And there were-- I wrote in my book about it-- that Haim Landau came to see me, that I should take-- so Haim Landau, at that time, went to Israel. 

He became later, as you have maybe heard, he became later the chief of staff of the Irgun during the war against England. And he was a member of the, a member of this-- of the Knesset, a member of the Parliament. 

And he was later a minister. He was one of the-- he was one of the greatest orators in Israel, this Haim Landau. So he came to me together with another delegation. 

As a matter of fact, I didn't remember the other people who was there, and, and maybe I told-- I'll show you later a letter from an English writer, a writer in London. Rafael here-- Rafael Scharf, Rafael Scharf. He was in this delegation just the same. 

Now, he went to England. And he, he lives now in England. And he's a-- he's a, a quite a known-- a well-known writer and intellectual. As a matter of fact, there is a book, written by Anne Karpf. Maybe you have seen it, you have seen this book. I got this book from the library. 

This book is called Another War. It is written by Anne Karpf. She is a columnist at The Guardian, Guardian columnist, an intellectual. And she was a child of survivors. Her mother was a concert pianist in Berlin, Philadelphia-- in the, in the Berlin Symphony Orchestra. 

Her father was a the consular at the embassy, at the Polish Embassy in London and defected. And the-- but, but they-- they-- she was a child of survivors. It's a very interesting book, because that's the only book with a deep knowledge of psychology. 

To see the chasm and the love, the love and hatred between the survivor, the child, with-- with-- between the child of survivor and their parents. It's a beautiful book. I think Felicity is going to review this book. 

It's here in the library-- Heinemann, Heinemann. But in the-- in the introduction-- in the acknowledgment, she thanks her father, Scharf, for his help. And in the second part, she writes quite a-- quite a few pages about this-- her father, Scharf, who was, at that time, the, the member of this delegation in Cracow, who came to me, who came to me that I should take over. 

The only thing is, you see, I went into hiding in Poland. And Scharf was clever enough. Haim Landau went to Israel to become later a minister. And Rafael Scharf went to England, where he studied at Oxford and served in the, and served in the British army and later became one of the intellectuals, the Jewish intellectuals in, in London. 

He is, he was one of the founders of the Jewish Quarterly. 

INT: How long, how long did you remain president in Cracow of the Jewish organization? 

MV: Until the end of my studies. Until the end of my studies, I was the president of the Jewish, of this Jewish organization. And there were other organizations. 

There were fraternities, [NON-ENGLISH] fraternities, the same fraternities as the Polish, as the Polish corporations-- [NON-ENGLISH] corporations, or fraternities. 

I used to, they used to invite me for lectures. And I used to lecture very often at the [NON-ENGLISH]. And interestingly, I received from a fellow, here in Australia, called Alexander, a photo-- a photocopy from a book, written about the [NON-ENGLISH.] 

And there, they claimed that I was a member. I must have been an honorary member. Yeah, I've got it here. They made me a member, but I was never a member of the fraternities. 

INT: During that time, at the end of your university studies, 1935, '36, as a young man of very strong Jewish tradition, background, did you feel that being Jewish was an issue in the day to day life around you? 

MV: Sure, it was an issue. We knew in advance it was an issue. But we accepted it. We knew in advance that the Constitution, giving us full rights, was just a fiction, though we accepted it. We accept. 

For instance, the Poles killed themselves to get honors at the university, because with that, with the honors, they will get better positions. The Jew knew in advance that he will not get any position, any position. 

INT: Why was that? 

MV: Because, because, because he was Jewish. And he couldn't get out of it. You know, the system in Poland was such that when you lived in a town, you were registered there. And there was a column stating your religion and your nationality. 

You could have said your nationality is Poland, but your religion is Jewish. You couldn't change your religion. You couldn't, even, even if you were an atheist, you were born a Jew, and it is written there. 

If you want-- and besides-- and because of that, you belong to the Jewish kahal, to the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] to to, to the Jewish community. And the Jewish community could impose taxes on you. 

And if you didn't pay the taxes, the sheriff, the government sheriff, will come and collect your candlesticks and sell them at an auction to pay the Jewish taxes. Now, you may think, if I am running away from my shtetl in Mielec to Cracow, I'll be able to say, I'm not Jewish. 

Can't do it, because according to the Polish regulations, if you-- if, if you went-- if, if you migrated from Mielec-- not only migrated, if you went for, for a holiday from Mielec to Cracow for four weeks, you had to fill out a slip, where it is written-- I, Mark Verstandig, nationality, Polish, or nationality, Jewish-- whatever you like to write, and religion, Jewish, or Jewish religion, and so on-- I'm going to Cracow. 

And you can't, when you came to Cracow, you had to come to the city council and fill out a new, a new slip, and there it was written, you showed. It is written there. The full slip said you are Jewish. 

You couldn't say that you are not Jewish, because the moment you are born a Jew, the only way of getting out of it was conversion. If you did not convert, you stayed a Jew. You could have shouted to heaven, I don't believe in God. You are still a Jew. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next time. 

MV: Next time? But we had to--
